Time to remind everyone what the technical feasible minimum is for refineries. If you see reports of 60% utilization, that already means the refinery is running as low as they can. 

It's zero or min 60% as a rule of thumb.

Nobody likes shutting down units. Restarting takes up a significant resources, costs money and exposes the site to operational/safety risks.

Refineries would probably still take some hit to buy crude at slightly negative margins to keep the refinery running at min.

Let's rationalize what will happen to NWE refineries. 

Margins are bad for replenishment cargoes - that's for the end April/early May North Sea grades, with WTI still positive margin. Crude premiums aren't going to come off in a big way since Asia is bidding up for the same arb cargoes now.

So runcuts for that time period sounds likely, maybe load down a bit now already since you want to stretch out your cheaper prompt feedstock a bit longer. 

Cracks and product time spreads are already very high and seem to be subject to the whims of headline risks. The only solver here seems to be higher physical premiums on products to nudge that margins back to at least breakeven levels.

Not enough diesel and jet fuel, you say? Well the refining system in Europe is not going to produce enough anyway to offset the losses from the Strait of Hormuz closure. The runcuts will just make things worse. Welcome to the world of Asian refinery, mirrored one month later in Europe and probably amplified in the diesel and jet fuel prices in this very short region. 

P/s: Did anyone forget about multiple Russian refineries also getting attacked by Ukrainian drones?

London’s Coal Supply in the 17th Century – A Case Study in Energy Security

By the early seventeenth century, coal had replaced wood as the main fuel used in London and other towns in southeast England for domestic heating as well as most manufacturing, other than iron-making.  

Nearly all of London’s coal came from fields in northeast England, where it was loaded onto vessels at the ports of Newcastle and Sunderland, carried down the east coast and then up the River Thames to be unloaded in the capital.  

Coal had become critical for energy security and national defence and therefore a preoccupation for top policymakers. 

Disruptions to the coal supply resulted in hardship and in some cases even death for lower-income households who had no alternatives for heating and cooking. 

Senior officials in the City of London as well as the national government repeatedly expressed fears that fuel shortages and extremely high prices would result in unrest rioting among the poorer classes. 

Lack of coal was also a potential national security issue because of the threat to the production of gunpowder and other items essential for the defence of the realm. 

“Now that London had become dependent upon the north of England for her fuel, any English government must retain control of Newcastle as the price of its existence, and the nation must have a navy strong enough to keep the enemy from blocking the passage along the east coast,” according to one of the foremost historians of Britain’s coal industry. 

London’s coal supply from Newcastle down the east coast was as important for energy security in the seventeenth century as the oil supply from the Middle East via the Strait of Hormuz is in the twenty-first century. 

Many of the policy responses to energy security challenges in the seventeenth century were the same as those commonly used today ...

NORTH SEA FORTIES crude prices closed at a record $147 per barrel on April 9. Forties is one of the principal streams in the Brent complex and prices have doubled since before the war between the United States and Iran broke out. Prices have remained high since the ceasefire reflecting the continued scramble for available barrels in the physical market and intense squeeze on North Sea supply. Forties prices have averaged $139 per barrel so far this month but remain slightly below the previous high of $144 in June 2022 (after Russia’s invasion of Ukraine) and well below the record of $205 in June 2008 after adjusting for inflation:

Another 4.5-5 million b/d is at risk. 

Suez is possible, but it would take too long.

Keep in mind that Iran can divert crude production to Jask. 

This won’t be that easy for the US.
Sellside analysts will start publishing reports about how high oil prices have to get to offset 13-15 million b/d of supply outage.

One thing people may not know is that Iran has been holding floating storage in the Gulf of Oman. 

They have prepared for the possibility of a Naval blockade. They have also waited to sell oil. 

Now that the blockade is here, Iran can use the floating storage to sell the oil to get revenue. This will buy them time. 

In the meantime, oil prices are headed for demand destruction. This will now test which side has more resolve. Can the US really stomach prices that create COVID level demand destruction before the Iranian run out of money?

I don’t know, but it’s going to get messy. 

But if you think the Iranians will fold right away, it won’t be that easy.

The other issue now is that the Strait of Hormuz blockade will introduce a possible Houthis involvement in the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, that’s 4.5-5 million b/d of export. 

If Iran loses 1.6-2 and we lose another 4.5-5 on top, global supply outage would reach 17.1 to 18 million b/d.

The Naval blockade fits in the category of escalate to de-escalate. 

It was going to get worse before it gets better. 

The blockade will knock out 1.6-2 million b/d of Iranian crude/condensate exports. 

Oil market breaking point was an inevitable outcome, so I think it’s a good move to just make it dramatically worse first before coming to a resolution. 

From an oil price standpoint, demand destruction is an inevitability now. 

Now we watch all the signals for oil demand destruction to rebalance the market.

For the oil market, these are the only 5 charts that matter right now.

All eyes on the first Iranian tanker to test Trump’s blockade.

Iran has thus far left most upstream oil production assets and export infrastructure untouched, but that’s Tehran’s next obvious escalatory ramp should the US Navy attack/seize an Iranian tanker.

More restrained than Trump’s blanket Hormuz blockade announced this morning.

CENTCOM: “The blockade will be enforced impartially against vessels of all nations entering or departing Iranian ports and coastal areas, including all Iranian ports on the Arabian Gulf and Gulf of Oman. CENTCOM forces will not impede freedom of navigation for vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz to and from non-Iranian ports.”

Bunch of people have asked so to answer directly: these Hormuz offset numbers are entirely bunk.

Absolutely fair to be optimistic about the war ending and Hormuz reopening, but it’s fundamentally flawed to say we’re offset the Hormuz supply loss and irresponsible to clam it’s not a serious ongoing crisis.

Bad estimates (the reality below)

🇸🇦 7M: Saudi Reroute
(That’s total East -West pipeline capacity, already had 2-2.5 on the line so remaining “swing” from Gulf to Red Sea is 4.5-5.0 MMbpd)

📈 4.25M: Pre-War Surplus
(We did have a pre-war surplus, but it was closer to 2 MMbpd, and even that remains disputed—I was on the bearish side of the debate)

🇨🇳 2M: China Safe-Passage
(There is no steady cleared safe passage)

🇦🇪 1.5M: UAE ADCOP reroute
(Again, this is the pipe capacity—swing is more like 0.5-0.7)

🇮🇷 1M: Iran Jask Bypass
(This is silly, Jask never demonstrated that capacity but more fundamentally Iran Hormuz flows actually remain higher than that at 1.5+ MMbpd)

🇮🇳 400k: India Safe-Passage
(There is no steady cleared safe passage)

In reality, we have ~13 MMbpd of upstream Gulf production offline, with no sustainable offset—SPRs, etc. are only a temporary help.

Today’s Trump blockade would raise that to more than 15 MMbpd.

Iran must end all uranium enrichment.

The dismantling of all major nuclear enrichment facilities.

The retrieval of highly enriched material.

A fully reopened Strait of Hormuz that is free of tolls.

On a day when Iranian and U.S. officials have exchanged fiery threats and accusations over the failed talks in Islamabad this weekend, Iran's ambassador to Pakistan aired a far less alarmist position in a social media post on Sunday evening.

Reza Amiri Moghadam, who was a senior member of the Iranian delegation at the talks, said that the Islamabad Talks should not be seen as an event “but a process.”

 https://t.co/43CxdS9gMJ

Iran’s Speaker of the Parliament Mohammad-Bagher Ghalibaf said Sunday that recent U.S. threats “have no effect” on Iranians, adding that “this is not a slogan—we have proven it.”

Speaking to reporters upon returning from Islamabad, he warned Washington against testing Iran’s resolve again, saying it would face “an even bigger lesson.”

Ghalibaf said Iran has not trusted the United States for 77 years, accusing the U.S. of attacking Iran twice in less than a year. “Therefore, it is they who must earn our trust,” he said.

He added that rebuilding trust would be difficult and time-consuming due to repeated breaches, and said the U.S. had shown little willingness to do so in the latest round of talks.

Yesterday (2026-04-11), the Strait of Hormuz saw the departure of 2 million barrels of Iraqi crude oil and 4 million barrels of Saudi crude oil. One of the three VLCC supertankers is under Greek ownership while the other two are under Chinese ownership. #OOTT #IranWar #Tankers

This month, Iran has chosen to keep 23 million barrels of crude oil afloat at all times in the Gulf of Oman, even as their tankers rotate in and out of the country. They are doing so while also maintaining fairly strong exports. #OOTT #IranWar #Tankers https://t.co/3Rvw3L7lCv

FAQ: “Why?”

We believe that Iran has moved its floating oil storage to the Gulf of Oman so that it can better regulate exports amid turbulence. They are also positioning these barrels as far away as possible from Kharg in case something does impact tanker loadings at the island.

From what we can see, 133 tankers have departed the Strait of Hormuz over the past 41 days since 2026-03-01. That's only 3.24 tankers a day.

Of that total, 90 are known sanctions violators which wouldn't be paying any tolls to Iran.

Among the remaining 43 tankers, we see 3 VLCC supertankers, 1 LNG tanker and 27 LPG tankers.

Iraq's oil exports remain abysmal. So much for "brotherly" relations.

#OOTT #Tankers #IranWar

There were 27 LPG tankers in total, out of which 18 are sanctions violators.

NYT: 3 main sticking points remained, according to two Iranian officials familiar with the talks: 

- the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz; Iran saying it would do so only after a final peace deal

- the fate of nearly 900 pounds of highly enriched uranium. Iran made a counterproposal, but the sides were unable to reach a compromise, the officials said. 

- Iran’s demand that about $27 billion in frozen revenues held abroad be released.

The meeting between U.S. and Iranian officials, as expected or feared, yielded no tangible outcome, reflecting the fact that the U.S. is not negotiating from a position of strength. The lack of progress risks pushing the global market closer to a full-blown energy crisis.

Crude carriers are now being redirected from the Middle East to the U.S. to lift barrels, but this adjustment will take time. Moreover, not all crude grades are suitable for producing the most urgently needed refined products such as diesel, jet fuel, and petrochemicals, limiting the effectiveness of this response.

Concerns are already surfacing in Europe. Airports Council International (ACI) Europe has warned that the region could face jet fuel rationing within weeks. In a letter to the EU’s energy and transport commissioners, the organisation highlighted that the bloc may be as little as three weeks away from supply shortages.

From a U.S. policy perspective, the options remain constrained: a retreat, which would carry significant political cost; renewed military action, with no clear endgame; or an extension of the ceasefire to allow further negotiations. The latter appears the most likely near-term outcome, as Washington adjusts to a stronger Iranian position. Control over the Strait of Hormuz continues to provide Tehran with significant leverage, and there is little indication it will accept terms that weaken its strategic standing.

Overall, the situation remains highly uncertain, with limited visibility on a near-term resolution and a clear risk of further market disruption.

COT on #forex showed a 53% jump in gross USD longs versus eight IMM futures to $17.5 billion - a 14-month high - up from a $19 billion short just before the Middle East conflict began, highlighting a sharp shift into the most liquid safe-haven asset. 

This buying occurred despite a small net loss in the dollar on the week, with that weakness accelerating following the Iran–US ceasefire announcement.

All eight currency contracts saw net selling, led by CAD ($1.7bn equivalent), JPY ($1.6bn), and EUR ($1.2bn). This resulted in the overall euro position flipping to a 7.5k contract short - the first in 14 months, and down from a February high at +180k contracts, a €23.5 billion change.

COT on #commodities:
The week to 7 April saw the BCOM Index rise 2% with gains in livestock and not least energy offsetting weakness in precious metals, grains and softs. Besides the mentioned activity in energy, the following stood out:

Gold length cut to 92k contracts, a 25-month low when bullion traded just above $2,000/oz
Soybean oil net long reached another all-time high at 150.7k contracts driven by its bio-fuel link to surging fuel prices. From a four-year high, the grains sector overall suffered its first week of net selling in 12 weeks.
Cocoa short hit a November 2022 high at 16.4k
Cotton short cut to near neutral and smallest in two years.

More in my Monday update

COT on #crudeoil: Managed money maintained a total net long near a four-year high in the week to 7 April, just ahead of the US–Iran ceasefire announcement. This likely helps explain part of the subsequent 16% price slump, reinforcing the view that last week’s sharp decline was primarily driven by an overcrowded long rather than any meaningful easing in underlying fundamentals, which continue to point to a tightening physical market.

Across benchmarks, positioning developments were mixed but remained elevated overall. In WTI (ICE + CME), the net long increased by 5.5k to 109.2k contracts, while Brent saw a modest 5.6k reduction to 424.3k, leaving the combined total near a four-year high at 533.5k contracts. A long-short ratio in Brent at 11.3, compared with just 2.6 in WTI, underscores that Brent remains the preferred contract for expressing tightness.

While the ceasefire prompted a sharp correction in the paper market, it has yet to materially alter the physical backdrop. Key indicators - such as elevated prompt spreads and the continued premium in Dated Brent relative to futures - highlight ongoing and rising supply constraints. As such, any additional downside from here is likely to reflect further positioning clean-out rather than a deterioration in core market fundamentals.

Meanwhile, net selling was observed across the three diesel and gasoline futures contracts, as elevated volatility - and the associated need to reduce risk - more than offset the typical buy-into-strength behaviour exhibited by leveraged traders. https://t.co/z2xa57cChs

It is worth noting that while SHFE-monitored copper stockpiles have declined sharply in recent weeks, inventories in London have continued to build, reaching a 12-year high of 393 kt this week. As a result, the overall drawdown in total exchange-monitored stocks has remained modest, with a decline of around 7.5 kt this week, following a larger 89 kt reduction over the previous two weeks.

The #copper market continues to stabilise following last month’s sell-off, which was driven by rising growth concerns amid surging energy prices. While those concerns persist, the fundamental outlook is showing signs of improvement. China remains a key driver, with exchange-monitored stockpiles falling 39% over the past four weeks from a record high, while import premiums have climbed to a ten-month high, signalling robust demand.

The HG Copper contract has bounced strongly after - just like gold - finding buyers near the 200-day moving average, last at $5.3030.  Currently challenging the 50-day ma at $5.761 and the 0.382 retracement of the Feb to March correction. https://t.co/VitF81IEvi

#Agriculture: CBOT #wheat futures dropped to a one-month low, down 4% on the week, pressured by profit-taking and technical selling following the recent decline in energy prices. The move also comes after the USDA raised its forecast for end-season global wheat stocks to 283.1 million tons, up from 277 million previously. After holding a net short position for a record duration, hedge funds flipped to a small net long in the week to 31 March, leaving the market exposed to the recent deterioration in both technical and fundamental conditions.

North Sea crude prices for immediate delivery continue to highlight mounting stress in the physical market, as European and Asian refiners scramble to secure cargoes to replace volumes lost during the month-long blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.

Dated Brent—a key benchmark for physical North Sea shipments—has moved sharply into focus given the signal it provides on overall market tightness. It rose 7% yesterday to USD 132, while the June Brent futures contract lingered around USD 95, underscoring the growing dislocation between prompt supply and forward pricing.

Data from LSEG, cited by the Financial Times, show Forties Blend—another marker for immediate delivery—trading close to USD 147 per barrel on Thursday, further highlighting the acute strain in spot markets.

While the rest of world is reeling from disruption of gas supplies from Qatar, U.S. #natgas futures hit a 17-month low as high production levels and mild weather drive a growing surplus. The latest storage report from the EIA showed a 50 bcf increase to 1,911 bcf, 4.8% above the five-year average.
